Until someone audits the data.

1. Source traceability

Can every number in your ESG report be traced back to a reliable source?

Foundation

Every ESG metric has a documented source system or owner.

Examples: ERP, HR system, utility bill, supplier submission, travel record or operations platform.

High risk

Source files are stored in a controlled location.

Evidence is not sitting in personal inboxes, desktop folders or unmanaged spreadsheets.

High risk

Each reported figure can be linked to its original evidence.

The team can show how a dashboard value connects back to source data.

Medium

Data lineage is documented from source to disclosure.

The path from collection, calculation, review and reporting is clear.

Medium

2. Data quality

Is your ESG data complete, current and consistent enough to support credible disclosure?

Credibility

Completeness checks are performed before reporting.

Missing sites, suppliers, business units or periods are flagged before the report is finalised.

High risk

Data is updated on a defined reporting cadence.

Monthly, quarterly or annual updates are planned and monitored.

Medium

Calculation methods are standardised across business units.

Different teams are not using different formulas for the same metric.

High risk

Data quality issues are logged and corrected.

Exceptions, corrections and restatements are documented.

Useful

3. Scope 3 readiness

Can your organisation prove value-chain emissions beyond its own operations?

Biggest gap

Material Scope 3 categories have been identified.

Suppliers, logistics, product use, travel, waste and end-of-life categories are assessed for relevance.

High risk

Supplier data is collected directly where possible.

The organisation is not relying only on assumptions or outdated estimates.

High risk

Estimates are clearly marked and justified.

Where estimates are used, the methodology and reason are documented.

Medium

Scope 3 data owners are assigned across the value chain.

Procurement, logistics, operations and finance know what they own.

Medium

4. Governance and ownership

Is accountability clear enough for ESG data to be trusted?

Control

Each ESG pillar has an accountable business owner.

Environmental, Social and Governance data ownership is not vague or shared by default.

High risk

Approval workflows are documented.

Data collection, review, approval and sign-off steps are clear.

Medium

Roles and responsibilities are understood across departments.

Finance, Operations, HR, Procurement and Sustainability know their ESG data role.

Medium

Management reviews ESG data before external disclosure.

Leadership does not only review the narrative; they review the data foundation.

Useful

5. Audit evidence

Can you defend your ESG figures under scrutiny?

Verification

Audit trails exist for key ESG metrics.

The team can show what changed, who changed it, when it changed and why.

High risk

Evidence packs can be produced for priority disclosures.

Reports are supported by evidence, not only dashboard screenshots.

High risk

Sign-off records are retained.

Final approvals are documented by owner, date and version.

Medium

Known gaps are captured in an improvement roadmap.

Weak areas are prioritised before an auditor, investor or regulator asks for them.

Medium
